title: JVM & Performance
description: Class loading, JIT compilation, GC selection, memory areas, and diagnosing performance problems in a running JVM.

questions.question: Describe the JVM class loading process: loading, linking (verification, preparation, resolution) and initialization. What is the class loader delegation model and what problem does it prevent?
questions.answer:
Loading loads file with .class suffix creating a class. 
Then it verifies it (if it's valid) . 

During the initialization process we initialize all static members / class / fields with value. 


JAva class loader delegation takes parent' priority. 
So we 3 class loaders: Bootstrap, Platform, Application. Application has the highest prio - if it doesn't find class it let its child (platform to find it). 

This way we avoid spoofing classes by some malicious packages.

evaluation.feedback: Loading and initialization are essentially right: loading reads the .class bytes and creates the Class object, and initialization runs static initializers and assigns static field values. Two gaps in linking: you named verification but skipped preparation (static fields are created and set to DEFAULT values — 0/null/false — before any code runs) and resolution (symbolic references in the constant pool are resolved to actual classes, methods and fields, often lazily). The delegation direction is inverted: it is parent-FIRST. Application delegates UP to Platform, which delegates UP to Bootstrap; a loader only attempts the load itself if its PARENT cannot find the class. So Bootstrap effectively has the highest priority, not Application — and that is exactly why the spoofing protection you mention works: a java.lang.String on your classpath is never consulted, because Bootstrap always finds the real one first.

questions.followUp: Initialization is lazy. Name two triggers that force a class's static initializers (<clinit>) to run, and one situation where a class is loaded and linked but never initialized. Bonus: what is the difference between ClassNotFoundException and NoClassDefFoundError?

questions.question: Java is 'compiled to bytecode, then JIT-compiled'. Explain tiered compilation: what do the interpreter, C1 and C2 each contribute, and what is deoptimization — when does the JVM throw compiled code away?
questions.answer:
JIT is C1 - only parts of it is keeped there. I don't know what is deoptimization but I believe that if there's too much bytecode in C1 it goes into runtime. 

PLEASE WRITE A HUGE DESCRIPTION ABOUT C1, C2, INTERPRETED CODE WITH SECTIONS, EXAMPLES AND KEY WORDS IN ASSIST SECTION.

evaluation.feedback: Honest about the gap, but what is there is off. The JIT is not 'C1 only' — tiered compilation uses the interpreter, C1 AND C2 together: every method starts interpreted while counters profile it, warm methods get a fast lightly-optimized C1 compile (which keeps profiling), and hot methods get an aggressive profile-guided C2 compile. Deoptimization is not about 'too much bytecode' — it is the JVM throwing compiled code away and falling back to the interpreter when a speculative assumption baked into that code turns out to be false (e.g. a branch profiled as never-taken suddenly fires, or loading a new class breaks a monomorphic call-site assumption). The full picture is in the explanation below.

questions.followUp: C2 sees from the profile that only one class implements an interface, so it devirtualizes and inlines the call. What is this kind of optimization called, what exactly happens at the moment a second implementation is class-loaded, and why is making such breakable assumptions still a net win?
questions.explanation:
The big picture: javac compiles Java to bytecode. At runtime HotSpot executes that bytecode in three escalating modes — interpreter, C1, C2 — promoting each method up the tiers as it proves itself hot. This is tiered compilation.

Tier 0 — the interpreter: executes bytecode instruction-by-instruction. Slow per instruction, but starts instantly and, crucially, collects profiling data: invocation counters, loop back-edge counters, which branches are taken, and which concrete types appear at call sites.

Tiers 1–3 — C1, the client compiler: once a method gets warm (its counters cross a threshold), C1 compiles it to native code fast, with only light optimizations (basic inlining, constant folding). Tier-3 C1 code keeps profiling instrumentation in, so the JVM keeps learning while already running much faster than interpreting.

Tier 4 — C2, the server compiler: for truly hot methods. Slow to compile, but produces heavily optimized code driven by the gathered profile: aggressive inlining, loop unrolling, escape analysis, vectorization — and speculative optimizations that assume the profile stays true ('this branch is never taken', 'only one class ever implements this interface'). Compiled code is stored in the code cache.

Deoptimization: speculative code carries guards called uncommon traps. When an assumption breaks, the trap fires, the JVM throws the compiled code away (marks it 'made not entrant'), transfers the running frame back to the interpreter, re-profiles, and later recompiles under the new reality.

interface Shape { double area(); }   // only Circle is loaded so far

double total(List<Shape> shapes) {
  double sum = 0;
  for (Shape s : shapes)
    sum += s.area(); // C2: monomorphic -> devirtualized + inlined, no virtual call
  return sum;
}

// later: new Square() gets class-loaded
//  -> assumption 'only Circle implements Shape' is now false
//  -> uncommon trap fires -> method DEOPTIMIZED back to the interpreter
//  -> re-profiled -> recompiled with a real virtual dispatch

Key words:

  • OSR (on-stack replacement) — swapping a long-running loop onto compiled code mid-execution, without waiting for the method to be re-entered
  • Code cache (-XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize) — where JIT output lives; if it fills up, compilation stops and performance degrades
  • Monomorphic vs megamorphic call sites — one receiver type (inlinable) vs many (plain virtual dispatch)
  • -XX:+PrintCompilation — watch methods climb the tiers and spot made not entrant on deopt

Takeaway: interpreter = start instantly and gather facts; C1 = quick, decent code; C2 = gamble on the facts for maximum speed; deoptimization = the safety net that makes the gamble safe.

questions.question: Map out the JVM's memory areas: heap, thread stacks, metaspace, code cache, and direct (off-heap) memory. For each, name one error or symptom you see when it is exhausted.
What is escape analysis and what optimizations does it enable (scalar replacement, lock elision)? Why can allocating short-lived objects in a hot loop still be effectively free?
Compare the modern garbage collectors: G1, ZGC and Shenandoah. What does each optimize for, and how would you choose one for (a) a latency-sensitive API with a 100 GB heap and (b) a small batch job?

A service's p99 latency spikes every few minutes while p50 stays flat. Walk through how you would confirm or rule out GC as the cause — which logs/flags and which metrics (pause time vs allocation rate) would you look at?
Which JDK tools would you use on a live, misbehaving JVM and for what: `jcmd`, `jstack`, `jmap`, `jstat`? Describe how you would diagnose a suspected deadlock and a suspected memory leak with them.

What is Java Flight Recorder (JFR), and why is it safe to run in production? What kinds of events does it capture, and how does it compare to a sampling profiler like async-profiler with flame graphs?
Why do JVM applications start slow, and what do CDS/AppCDS, AOT caching (Project Leyden) and GraalVM native image each do about it? What do you trade away with a native image?

Why are naive Java microbenchmarks (a loop around `System.nanoTime()`) almost always wrong? Explain the pitfalls — warmup, dead-code elimination, on-stack replacement — and how JMH is designed to avoid them.
